1: "Now this is the commandment — the statutes and the rules — that the LORD your God commanded me to teach you, that you may do them in the land to which you are going over, to possess it" — the transition from the Decalogue (Deuteronomy 5) to the law code proper: Deuteronomy 6 introduces the great interpretive principle (the Shema, vv4-5) that governs all the laws that follow. The land-destination framing is deliberate: this is law for the land, not for the wilderness. The stipulations are oriented toward sedentary life — farming, urban living, regular worship at a central sanctuary.
2: "That you may fear the LORD your God, you and your son and your son's son, by keeping all his statutes and his commandments, which I command you, all the days of your life, and that your days may be long" — the three-generation formulation: you, your son, your son's son. Covenant faithfulness is inherently multigenerational. The law is not an individual matter but a family-and-community project requiring transmission across generations. The "all your days" temporal scope means the covenant claims the entirety of life, not just formal religious occasions.
3: "Hear therefore, O Israel, and be careful to do them, that it may go well with you, and that you may multiply greatly, as the LORD, the God of your fathers, has promised you, in a land flowing with milk and honey" — the promised-land description: "a land flowing with milk and honey" (eretz zavat chalav udvash) appears 20 times in the Pentateuch and is the signature description of Canaan's agricultural abundance. Archaeologically, the Syro-Palestinian region supported both pastoral livestock (milk) and beekeeping/flowering plants (honey). The phrase is formulaic but grounded in the actual agricultural potential of the Levantine land compared to the desert Israel was leaving.
4: "Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God, the LORD is one" — the Shema (from shema, hear/listen): this six-word declaration (in Hebrew: shema Yisrael YHWH eloheinu YHWH echad) became the central confession of Judaism, recited twice daily. Its reception in the ANE world: the Canaanite city-states each had their own ba'al (lord/master), typically identified with Baal Hadad or El. The Philistines had Dagon. The Moabites had Chemosh. The Ammonites had Molech. The Shema asserts that the God of Israel is not one regional deity among many but the only LORD — a revolutionary claim in a world of territorial polytheism. The Ugaritic texts show the complexity of Canaanite divine bureaucracy; the Shema sweeps it away with six words.
5: "You shall love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your might" — the ahavah (love) command: love of the deity was not a common feature of ANE religious expression, which focused on propitiation, service, and fear. Deuteronomy's command to love YHWH is distinctive. The three dimensions — heart (levav), soul (nefesh), and might (meod) — comprehensively cover the rational-volitional center, the animate life-self, and the material resources. Mark 12:30 adds a fourth dimension ("mind") by distinguishing levav and dianoia in the Greek. The Shema-love command is the summary of the entire Torah (as Jesus teaches in Matthew 22:37-40).
6: "And these words that I command you today shall be on your heart" — the internalization imperative: the law is not merely an external code but something to be carried inside the person. The lev (heart) is the center of decision-making and intention in Hebrew anthropology. The law on the heart is the precursor to Jeremiah 31:33's new covenant promise — but Deuteronomy 6:6 commands what Jeremiah 31:33 promises as a divine gift. The gap between the command (Deuteronomy 6:6) and the gift (Jeremiah 31:33) is the gap between old covenant demand and new covenant provision.
7: "You shall teach them diligently to your children, and shall talk of them when you sit in your house, and when you walk by the way, and when you lie down, and when you rise" — the total-life-embedding of Torah: four times of day (sitting, walking, lying down, rising up) cover the full round of daily life. Teaching children is not confined to formal instruction sessions but embedded in every daily activity. The home (beitecha) and the road (vederech) are both Torah-teaching spaces. The pedagogy is conversational and situational, not only formal-instructional. The Jewish practice of discussing Torah at the dinner table, on the Sabbath walk, and at bedtime derives directly from this verse.
8: "You shall bind them as a sign on your hand, and they shall be as frontlets between your eyes" — the phylactery command: totafot (H2903, frontlets/phylacteries) are the objects worn on the forehead; tefillin in later Jewish practice are leather boxes containing Torah passages (Exodus 13:1-16; Deuteronomy 6:4-9; Deuteronomy 11:13-21) worn on the arm and forehead during morning prayer. The command may be understood literally (as Jewish practice has), metaphorically (law always before your eyes/guiding your actions), or both. Matthew 23:5 criticizes the scribes and Pharisees for widening their phylactery boxes for show — confirming the practice was widespread by the first century CE.
9: "You shall write them on the doorposts of your house and on your gates" — the mezuzah tradition: a mezuzah (doorpost) scroll containing Deuteronomy 6:4-9 and Deuteronomy 11:13-21 was affixed to doorposts in Jewish homes. The command's literalizing produced one of Judaism's most visible and durable practices — the mezuzah has been an identifying mark of Jewish homes for millennia. Archaeologically, inscribed doorpost plaques have been found at ancient sites. The mezuzah practice embeds the covenant's claim into the physical structure of the home: YHWH's words mark the threshold, turning the domestic space into covenanted space.
10: "And when the LORD your God brings you into the land that he swore to your fathers, to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob, to give you — with great and good cities that you did not build" — the danger of prosperity: vv10-12 address the theological risk of inheriting developed civilization. The cities, houses, cisterns, vineyards, and olive trees were built and planted by others. The danger is that prosperity without struggle produces forgetting. This is one of the earliest recorded analyses of the spiritual danger of inherited wealth and civilizational comfort.
11: Houses filled with all good things that you did not fill, and cisterns that you did not dig, and vineyards and olive trees that you did not plant — the comprehensive list of inherited goods. Cisterns cut in bedrock were major infrastructure investments in the ancient Levant, where water was scarce. Vineyards and olive groves required decades of cultivation before becoming productive. Israel is about to inherit the infrastructure and agriculture of civilizations they did not build — a profound economic gift that carries proportional spiritual risk.
12: "Then take care lest you forget the LORD, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery" — the forget-warning: hishamer lecha pen tishkach (take care lest you forget). Forgetting YHWH is the root of all subsequent covenant failure in Deuteronomy's framework. The entire book of Judges demonstrates this pattern: Israel settles, prospers, forgets YHWH, worships Baal, suffers, cries out, YHWH raises a judge, delivers, and the cycle repeats. The forget-warning at the threshold of the land is the narrative setup for everything that follows in the Deuteronomistic History.
13: "It is the LORD your God you shall fear. Him you shall serve and by his name you shall swear" — the exclusive-service command: YHWH alone receives fear (yira), service (avad), and oath-backing (shevuah). Jesus quotes 6:13 to Satan in the wilderness temptation (Matthew 4:10; Luke 4:8): "You shall worship the Lord your God, and him only shall you serve" — the Shema's exclusive-service command becomes the hermeneutical lens for Jesus's rejection of demonic homage. Satan's offer of "all the kingdoms of the world" is precisely the kind of offer the Canaanite land-gift might become: prosperity in exchange for serving a different lord.
14: "You shall not go after other gods, the gods of the peoples who are around you" — the specific prohibition against the surrounding nations' gods: Baal (Canaanite storm god), Asherah (Canaanite goddess of fertility), Dagon (Philistine grain god), Chemosh (Moabite), Molech (Ammonite) are the specific deities Israel is warned against throughout the subsequent history. The Deuteronomistic History (Joshua through Kings) is structured as an account of how well Israel kept this command — the answer being "not well at all." The failure to maintain exclusive YHWH-worship is the recurring explanatory key for every national disaster.
15: "For the LORD your God in your midst is a jealous God (el qanna), lest the anger of the LORD your God be kindled against you, and he destroy you from the face of the earth" — the jealousy-warning amplifies the second commandment's jealousy language (Deuteronomy 5:9). The presence of YHWH in the community's midst is the source of both protection and danger: the same divine presence that delivers also punishes covenant violation. The spatial metaphor "in your midst" (beqirbecha) anticipates the tabernacle and temple's function as the place where YHWH's presence is concentrated within the community.
16: "You shall not put the LORD your God to the test, as you tested him at Massah" — the Massah reference: at Massah ("testing," Exodus 17:1-7) Israel demanded water and questioned whether YHWH was among them at all. The testing of YHWH is the application of empirical skepticism to covenant faith: treating YHWH's faithfulness as a hypothesis to be verified rather than a covenant reality to be trusted. Jesus quotes this verse to Satan (Matthew 4:7; Luke 4:12) in response to the temptation to jump from the temple pinnacle and summon angelic rescue — another form of demanding that God prove himself.
17: "You shall diligently keep the commandments of the LORD your God, and his testimonies and his statutes, which he has commanded you" — the threefold covenant-vocabulary: mitzvot (commandments), edut (testimonies/witness), chuqqim (statutes). The three terms appear together throughout Deuteronomy and the Psalms (Ps 119 uses all three and more). The distinctions are sometimes drawn: mitzvot as general commands, chuqqim as statutes without stated reasons (like the dietary laws), mishpatim as case-law. The threefold repetition emphasizes comprehensive covenant coverage: every category of divine instruction is to be kept.
18: "And you shall do what is right and good in the sight of the LORD, that it may go well with you, and that you may go in and take possession of the good land that the LORD swore to give to your fathers" — the summary principle: "right and good in the sight of the LORD" (hayashar vehatov be'einei YHWH) is an open-ended standard beyond specific law. Even where no specific commandment applies, the covenant person asks what is right and good before YHWH. This principle prevents the legalism of "anything not explicitly prohibited is permitted" — the covenant shapes character, not just compliance.
19: "By thrusting out all your enemies from before you, as the LORD has promised" — the land-possession is presented as YHWH's pushing-out of enemies (lehadof): Israel's military activity is secondary to YHWH's action. The same verb (dafah) is used in Exodus for YHWH's action against Egypt. Israel's conquest of Canaan is framed throughout Deuteronomy as YHWH's action, not Israel's military prowess (Deuteronomy 9:4-6: "not because of your righteousness").
20: "When your son asks you in time to come, 'What is the meaning of the testimonies and the statutes and the rules that the LORD our God has commanded you?'" — the catechetical question-and-answer: the son's question and the father's response (vv21-25) is the earliest recorded formal religious education format in the Bible. The Passover Haggadah formalizes this into four sons with four questions, reflecting this Deuteronomic pattern. The question-and-answer method ensures the covenant story is transmitted relationally, not merely textually — the next generation must ask, and the current generation must be ready to answer.
21: "Then you shall say to your son, 'We were Pharaoh's slaves in Egypt. And the LORD brought us out of Egypt with a mighty hand'" — the catechetical answer begins with the confession of slavery: the covenant history starts with the lowest point (slavery), not with the patriarchal glory. The starting point of humility and need is theologically significant: the covenant identity is not built on ethnic superiority but on received rescue. The Passover confession has this same structure: we were slaves, YHWH brought us out.
22: "And the LORD showed signs and wonders, great and grievous, against Egypt and against Pharaoh and all his household, before our eyes" — "before our eyes" (le'eineinu): the eyewitness claim is part of the catechetical answer. The father tells his son: we saw this. The participatory identity ("we were slaves," "we saw") creates covenantal solidarity across generations — the son inhabits the Exodus story as his own, not as a distant ancestor's story. This is the Deuteronomic contemporization principle in its catechetical form.
23: "And he brought us out from there, that he might bring us in and give us the land that he swore to give to our fathers" — the double movement of redemption: brought out (from Egypt) → brought in (to Canaan). The two movements define the Exodus as purposive: liberation was not the end goal but the means to the land-gift. This double-movement (exodus → entry) becomes the NT's hermeneutical framework: Christ brought us out (from sin/death) to bring us in (to the kingdom/rest). Hebrews 3-4 uses this framework explicitly.
24: "And the LORD commanded us to do all these statutes, to fear the LORD our God, for our good always, that he might preserve us alive, as we are this day" — the covenant's self-interest argument: obedience is "for our good" (letovatenu). The covenant is not primarily YHWH's benefit (he gains nothing from Israel's obedience) but Israel's — the statutes are designed as life-giving instructions, not arbitrary impositions. Paul uses similar logic in Romans 7:12 ("the law is holy, and the commandment is holy and righteous and good") — the law's goodness is not undermined by its inability to produce righteousness; it remains good even when humans are unable to keep it.
25: "And it will be righteousness for us, if we are careful to do all this commandment before the LORD our God, as he has commanded us" — the righteousness-by-Torah promise: covenant faithfulness produces righteousness (tzedaqah). This is the Torah's own promise — obedience to YHWH's commands constitutes the righteous life. Paul's argument in Romans 3:21-4:25 does not deny that Torah-righteousness was genuine righteousness for those who achieved it (Zacharias and Elizabeth in Luke 1:6 are described as "righteous before God, walking blamelessly in all the commandments") — his argument is that no one achieves it (Romans 3:10-18) and that a righteousness by faith was always available (Abraham's faith counted as righteousness, Genesis 15:6, before circumcision).
